text: The laparoscopic myomectomy begins with high port positioning near the xiphoid process. The surgeon pushes the uterovesical fold down, identifies the round ligament, and performs bilateral uterine artery ligation.

Following bilateral uterine artery ligation, the surgeon makes an incision over the most prominent part of the large anterofundal fibroid, approximately eighteen centimeters in size. The fibroid is dissected and pulled out from the underlying myometrial capsule. Minimal blood loss is observed in the pouch of Douglas, despite not using vasopressin, as the uterine arteries are clipped.

The surgeon places two clips over the uterine arteries on both sides, noting that the artery size remains under five millimeters even with large fibroids. After removing both fibroids, multi-layered suturing of the myoma bed is performed using quill sutures, which are knotless due to their barbed design. The posterior fibroid site is sutured in four layers, ensuring no hematoma formation, followed by suturing the anterior part. Minimal blood loss is observed without the use of suction irrigation.

After completing multi-layer suturing of the myoma beds, the surgeon uses a morcellator to extract the fibroid from the peritoneal cavity. This step is visually detailed and time-consuming, especially for fibroids weighing over two kilograms.
